2026 · arXiv · Post-training & RL

Not Every Rubric Teaches Equally: Policy-Aware Rubric Rewards for RLVR

A static weighted rubric conflates two different things: what should matter in the final answer, and what can teach the policy right now. POW3R keeps the human weights as the fixed evaluation target but reroutes GRPO's training pressure toward the criteria that currently separate rollouts — winning 24 of 30 base-policy/metric comparisons and reaching the same plateau 2.5–4× faster.

Tyagi, Guo, Rezaei, George, Mahmoud, Lee, Liu, He · Scale AI
venue arXiv citations 1 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of June 2026
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) works beautifully when success collapses onto a single checkable outcome — a math answer, a unit test. But most behaviors worth post-training on are vector-valued: long-form medical advice, scientific writing, coding help, and visually grounded reasoning all require an answer to be factually correct, complete, faithful to evidence, well-formatted, and on-instruction at the same time. Rubrics capture that structure — they decompose a response into prompt-specific criteria, each scored independently by an LLM judge — and rubric-based rewards have become the standard way to push RL past strictly verifiable domains.

The catch is that GRPO still needs a single scalar reward per rollout, so every rubric criterion must eventually be folded into one number. The common answer is a static weighted sum, and it bakes in a hidden assumption: that a criterion's human-assigned weight expresses both its desired importance in the final answer and its current usefulness as a training signal. Those are not the same thing.

Here is the mechanism. Under group-relative RL with outcome supervision, the per-token advantage is the rubric reward standardized within the rollout group:

Âi = R(oi) − mean(R)std(R) // a criterion j that every rollout passes (pj=1) or none passes (pj=0) adds the same constant wj·sj to every R(oi) — and a constant cancels in the numerator. Zero gradient.

So a saturated criterion (everyone passes) or a dead one (no one passes) contributes nothing to the advantage no matter how heavily a human weighted it. Only mixed criteria — verdicts that differ across the group, with variance vj=pj(1−pj)>0 — can actually teach the current policy. The authors test this directly: two frozen base policies (Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct and Gemma 3 12B-IT) on 1,300 prompts drawn from their multimodal dataset (MM) and HealthBench English (HB), judging every criterion on every rollout with GPT-5.4-mini.

The result is stark and stable across both policies and both domains: 17–26% of criteria are saturated and 20–33% are dead for a fresh policy, leaving only about half able to produce a contrastive gradient. Static aggregation therefore routes 45–51% of within-category training pressure onto criteria that cannot move the policy. And this is not a low-importance problem: human weight and rollout variance are essentially uncorrelated, and roughly half of the highest-weight criteria already carry vj=0. As the paper puts it — static weights tell you what should matter in the final answer, not which criteria can teach the current model.

// how it works

The method

POW3R (Policy-aware Rubric Reward) changes only the reward aggregation that happens before GRPO standardization. The rubric, the judge scores sj(o,q)∈[0,1], and the human weights wj all stay fixed — the evaluation target never moves. What changes is how within-category pressure is allocated across criteria.

It builds in two steps. First, a category-normalized baseline divides each rubric category's contribution by its total weight and averages over the populated categories, so a category with many criteria can't dominate one with few (relaxing the implicit assumption that categories hold comparable numbers of criteria). Then a per-criterion policy-aware factor αj — initialized at 1 — is updated after each epoch from the rollout group's own verdicts:

vj = pj(1−pj),   gj = √(vj+ε),   ρj = gjḡκ
α̂j = clip( (1−λ) + λ·ρj,  αmin, αmax ) // contrastiveness gj is the smoothed std of judge verdicts, normalized against the weighted within-category mean ḡκ; blended toward 1, clipped to [0.67, 1.5], then EMA-updated (βema=0.2)

The clip is the key design choice: criteria that currently separate rollouts get more weight, but saturated and dead criteria keep a learning floor rather than dropping to zero — so the method never permanently abandons a criterion that might become contrastive later. The reweighted weights w̃j = wj·αj then feed a final category-normalized sum:

Rdyn(o; q) = 1Kq  Σk 1W̃k(q)  Σj∈Ck w̃j sj(o, q) // w̃j = wj·αj. Category mass stays uniform and wj acts as a prior — if factors within a category are equal, Rdyn collapses back to the static category-balanced reward.

Crucially, the renormalization preserves the human-weight prior and keeps each category's total mass uniform, so POW3R reallocates pressure within categories without changing the cross-category objective. The same {αj} are applied to all G rollouts and fed straight into GRPO — it is a drop-in reward change with no optimizer modification. An offline replay confirms the local mechanism: POW3R moves 8–12 pp of pressure off zero-signal criteria and widens the pre-standardization rollout reward spread by 25–37% (Fig. 1b,c), so fewer groups collapse to the tied std(R)=0 regime where every advantage is zero.

// what they found

The results

POW3R is evaluated against four other reward constructions — base (no RL), binary (all-or-nothing), static scalar (the prior-work weighted sum), and category-balanced — under an identical GRPO recipe, on six base policies: Qwen3-VL-4B/8B-Instruct and Gemma 3 4B-IT on the 10k-task multimodal set (MM; 84,403 criteria across six categories), and Qwen3-4B/8B and Gemma 3 4B-IT on HealthBench English (HB). The training reward judge is GPT-5.4-nano; held-out responses are re-scored by GPT-5.4-mini to reduce train/eval entanglement (91–94% verdict agreement vs a high-effort reference). Each setting averages three runs.

The headline: POW3R wins the best score on 24 of the 30 base-policy/metric comparisons, sweeping every MM rubric-reward and strict-completion column and every HealthBench overall-reward column. The six misses are 4 of 6 external VLM benchmarks (which it is not trained against) plus the HB perfect-score column on two policies, where it trails the static or category-balanced reward by just 0.1–0.2 pp. The ordering Rdyn > Rcat > Rscalar > base holds in all six combinations, and the smallest POW3R gain over base is still +3.7 pp.

RewardMM rubric %MM strict %HB overall %
Base (no RL)42.614.728.0
Static scalar47.117.929.6
Category-balanced47.918.730.5
POW3R dynamic48.820.232.7

// MM columns = Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct (vision); HB column = Qwen3-4B (text-only). On HB, POW3R's +4.7 pp over base beats static's +1.6 and category-balanced's +2.5.

Two findings sharpen the story. First, on the (mean rubric reward, strict completion) plane POW3R is the top-right endpoint of every base-policy line — it Pareto-dominates all four alternatives on both objectives at once, so its partial-credit gains actually convert into more prompts where every required criterion is satisfied. Second, the per-category trajectories show this is not one-category over-optimization: POW3R leads on every rubric category throughout training, with the biggest gaps on the contrastive categories (Visual Perception, Visual Reasoning, Truthfulness, Content, Instruction Following) and a near-tie on Writing Style — because most style criteria are already passed by the base policy and offer little contrast for POW3R to act on. That is by design: where there is no disagreement to exploit, POW3R reduces to the static baseline.

The other payoff is sample efficiency at matched compute — same recipe, same prompt budget, same checkpointing. On Qwen3-VL-4B/MM, POW3R reaches a 46.0 dev-reward threshold at step 83 while the static scalar needs 249 (a 4.0× speed-up) and category-balanced needs 332; across thresholds the speed-ups run 2.5–4×, and POW3R is the only construction to cross a 50.0 dev threshold within the 664-step schedule. It separates from both static aggregations by the very first logged checkpoint.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The diagnostic is the strongest part of the paper: the saturated/dead/mixed decomposition is a clean, falsifiable account of why static rubric sums leak training signal, and it is verified three ways — the aggregate shares (Fig. 1a), the offline pressure-shift replay (Fig. 1b,c), and a prompt-by-prompt mechanism check (Fig. 2). The fix is principled and cheap, and the ablation ladder is honest. But the magnitude of the win, and what mediates it, deserve a harder look.

The control that matters is category-balanced (Rcat), because it already contains the category-normalization half of POW3R. Read against that control rather than against "static scalar," the dynamic factor is an increment: roughly +0.9–1.2 pp rubric reward and +1–1.5 pp strict completion on MM, and +0.8–2.2 pp overall on HB. Much of the eye-catching "+4.7 pp over base" is the category balancing, not the policy-aware reweighting the paper is named for. The authors are transparent about the ordering, but the framing bundles the two.

And everything is measured through the same family of LLM judges the method trains against. The training reward, the reported scores, and POW3R's reweighting factors are all estimated from GPT-5.4 verdicts — so any systematic verdict bias is not just inherited but amplified into where training pressure gets sent. 91–94% agreement is decent, yet a 6–9% binary-disagreement rate compounds across a 16-rollout × 84k-criterion loop. The external VLM benchmarks (HallusionBench, POPE, MathVista, RealWorldQA…) move <1 pp, which the paper reads as "no overfitting" — but it equally means the gains live almost entirely inside the rubric judge's distribution, with little independent capability transfer.

baselines

Fair and well-laid-out (binary → static → category-balanced → POW3R), and the binary baseline never beats base, a good sanity check. But the honest comparison is POW3R vs the category-balanced control, where the dynamic factor adds only ~1 pp of rubric reward.

contamination

Low risk for a training method — held-out splits, and a deliberate nano→mini judge swap between train and eval. The deeper entanglement is that the reward, the metric, and the reweighting all flow through GPT-5.4 judges.

what's missed

Scale is small (4B/8B policies, ≤664 steps) and the central MM dataset is in-house and unreleased, so the multimodal sweep — the bulk of the 24/30 — is not independently reproducible; only the HealthBench half is. Absolute rubric-point gains over the right control are modest.

would raise confidence

A clean ablation isolating the dynamic factor at matched category-normalization across all six policies; a human- or non-LLM-graded slice; a second public weighted-rubric dataset beyond HealthBench; and frontier-scale policies with longer schedules (both flagged by the authors).

// bottom line

A genuinely useful reframing — treat rubric aggregation as a training-time signal-routing problem, not a fixed preference statement — backed by a convincing mechanism and consistent, if incremental, gains. The dynamic reweighting's clearest practical payoff is the 2.5–4× sample efficiency, not the ~1 pp it adds over category balancing; and its biggest caveat is that the whole result is observed through the same LLM judges it optimizes, on a multimodal corpus nobody outside Scale can yet inspect.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. In rubric RL, human importance ≠ learnability. For a fresh policy 17–26% of criteria are saturated and 20–33% are dead, so a static weighted sum wastes 45–51% of within-category training pressure on criteria that cannot move the policy — and human weight is essentially uncorrelated with rollout variance.
  2. POW3R keeps the rubric, judge, and human weights as the fixed evaluation target, but reweights within-category pressure toward criteria that currently separate rollouts — a bounded, EMA-smoothed factor derived from judge-verdict variance, clipped so saturated/dead criteria keep a learning floor. It's a drop-in change to GRPO's scalar reward with no optimizer change.
  3. The win is real but should be read against the category-balanced control: POW3R Pareto-dominates on mean reward and strict completion (24/30 comparisons) and trains 2.5–4× faster, while the dynamic factor itself adds ~1 pp over category balancing — and every number is mediated by GPT-5.4 judges on an unreleased multimodal dataset.
